subsystem: Linux kernel VFS mount / namespace subsystem (fs/namespace.c). The function clone_private_mount() creates a private, longterm clone of an existing mount for an in-kernel consumer (notably overlayfs, which clones the lower/upper/work directory mounts). It takes a struct path, resolves the underlying struct mount, validates that the mount may legitimately be cloned into a private context, produces a fresh CL_PRIVATE clone, marks it with the internal mount namespace sentinel, and returns the new vfsmount (or an ERR_PTR).
provenance: Identified by naming and API surface rather than SPDX/copyright (none shown). The identifiers are unmistakably Linux VFS internals: clone_private_mount, real_mount(), struct mount / struct vfsmount / struct path, down_read/up_read(&namespace_sem), IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE(), check_mnt(), has_locked_children(), clone_mnt(..., CL_PRIVATE), MNT_NS_INTERNAL, ERR_PTR/ERR_CAST/IS_ERR, and kern_unmount referenced in the comment. This is fs/namespace.c. The sample header comment 'primevul_c_test_0241_fixed' and the corpus filename eval_0027_cwe200.c indicate this is the FIXED variant of a CWE-200 (information exposure) case, i.e. the hardened version that added the check_mnt/has_locked_children gating.
trust_boundary: clone_private_mount() is an in-kernel API, but its struct path argument is derived from user-controllable input. Its principal caller, overlayfs, builds these paths from mount options (lowerdir=, upperdir=, workdir=) supplied by whoever performs the mount(2) — increasingly reachable by unprivileged users inside a user namespace. The hostile-controllable elements are therefore path->mnt and path->dentry: an attacker chooses WHICH mount subtree gets cloned into a private, longterm mount. The security question is whether the routine lets a caller clone a subtree it should not be permitted to see or detach — specifically one containing mounts that a more-privileged namespace locked in place. namespace_sem protects the mount tree topology while the checks run.

what_the_code_does.code:
/* sample: primevul_c_test_0241_fixed */
struct vfsmount *clone_private_mount(const struct path *path)
what_the_code_does.explanation: The banner comment labels this as a 'fixed' sample. The signature declares clone_private_mount taking a const struct path * and returning a struct vfsmount *. In kernel convention a struct vfsmount * return that may encode an error is checked with IS_ERR/PTR_ERR. The const on the path means the function must not mutate the caller's path, only read path->mnt and path->dentry from it. The caller retains ownership of path; the returned vfsmount is a NEW reference the caller must eventually release (the comment at line 23 says via kern_unmount*()).

real_mount(path->mnt) converts the public struct vfsmount * embedded in the path into the internal struct mount * (via container_of), which exposes the topology fields the mount code needs (mnt_flags, mnt_ns, child list, etc.). old_mnt is the source subtree the caller wants cloned. new_mnt is declared but not yet initialised; it will hold the clone. Note old_mnt is computed before namespace_sem is taken, but it is only a pointer arithmetic derivation of an object the caller already holds a reference to via path.

Acquires the global namespace_sem reader lock. namespace_sem serialises reads and writes of the mount tree topology. Holding it read-locked here guarantees the subsequent property checks (unbindable flag, in-namespace check, locked-children walk) and the clone_mnt() operation observe a consistent, non-shifting mount tree — nobody can graft, move, or detach mounts underneath old_mnt while these decisions are made.

First gate: rejects mounts marked MNT_UNBINDABLE (mount --make-unbindable). An unbindable mount is explicitly declared by the administrator/namespace owner as one that may never be bind-mounted or cloned. Cloning it privately would defeat that guarantee, so the function bails to the invalid label, releasing the lock and returning -EINVAL.

Second gate: check_mnt(old_mnt) verifies that old_mnt belongs to the current task's mount namespace (mnt->mnt_ns == current->nsproxy->mnt_ns and is not the internal/anonymous namespace). This ensures the caller can only clone a mount it legitimately sees in its own namespace, not some foreign namespace's mount that happened to be reachable by pointer. Failing the check jumps to invalid.

Third and most security-critical gate: has_locked_children walks the children of old_mnt beneath path->dentry and returns true if any child mount is locked (MNT_LOCKED). Locked child mounts are ones a more-privileged context mounted over a directory to hide/protect its contents and which an unprivileged namespace is forbidden to unmount or peek under. If such a locked child exists in the subtree being cloned, the clone is rejected — this is precisely the CWE-200 information-exposure defence: a private clone would otherwise let the caller detach or see around the locked mount and read the underlying contents.

After all three gates pass, clone_mnt() creates the actual clone rooted at path->dentry within old_mnt, with the CL_PRIVATE flag. CL_PRIVATE produces a clone that is not part of any peer/propagation group and not attached to a namespace — a standalone longterm mount. clone_mnt runs while namespace_sem is still held (correct: it reads/links topology). Immediately after, up_read releases namespace_sem. Crucially the lock is dropped here BEFORE the error check on the next line, so the success path holds no lock past this point.

clone_mnt() can fail (e.g. -ENOMEM, or an internal policy rejection) and returns an ERR_PTR-encoded struct mount *. IS_ERR detects that encoded error; ERR_CAST re-casts the same error pointer to the struct vfsmount * return type without dereferencing it. Because up_read already ran at line 18, this early return does not leak namespace_sem. No new_mnt cleanup is needed because on error clone_mnt returned no object.

On success the clone is tagged by setting new_mnt->mnt_ns to the MNT_NS_INTERNAL sentinel, marking it as a kernel-internal longterm mount not owned by any user mount namespace; this both documents its lifetime (freed by kern_unmount*()) and keeps namespace bookkeeping (mnt_ns counting, umount propagation) from treating it as a real namespace member. The function returns &new_mnt->mnt, the embedded struct vfsmount inside the struct mount, i.e. the public handle the caller uses.

The shared error label reached from all three rejecting gates (lines 9, 12, 15). It releases namespace_sem (which is always held when a goto invalid executes, since the gates run after down_read and before up_read) and returns ERR_PTR(-EINVAL). This single-exit unlock pattern guarantees the lock is released exactly once on the rejection paths, mirroring the single up_read on the success path at line 18.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core contract of the fix and the CWE-200 exposure surface. path->dentry is attacker-selectable (overlayfs lowerdir/upperdir), so an attacker deliberately points clone_private_mount at a subtree that contains a locked child mount — one that a privileged namespace mounted to hide the directory beneath it. If this check were absent, weakened, or applied against the wrong dentry, clone_mnt(CL_PRIVATE) would produce a standalone clone from which the caller can detach or navigate around the locked child and read the underlying (hidden) files: an information-disclosure and mount-namespace-escape primitive. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that has_locked_children considers the ENTIRE subtree that clone_mnt will copy (rooted at path->dentry), so no locked descendant slips through, and that it runs under namespace_sem so the child set cannot change between the check and clone_mnt at line 17. In this fixed sample the gate is present and correctly ordered before the clone; the residual risk is entirely in the correctness of has_locked_children itself (not shown).

The unbindable and namespace-membership gates are the other half of the access-control contract. check_mnt confines the caller to cloning a mount that actually lives in its own mount namespace; without it, a subtree reachable only by pointer (e.g. from another namespace or an internal/anonymous mount) could be cloned, violating namespace isolation. IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE enforces the administrator's explicit 'never clone/bind this' policy. The failure mode if either is dropped or reordered after clone_mnt is a policy bypass: the clone is materialised first and the check comes too late. The invariant is that all three gates must precede clone_mnt() and all must be evaluated (no short-circuit that skips a later one) — satisfied here.

real_mount(path->mnt) is a container_of derivation that unconditionally treats path->mnt as a valid embedded vfsmount, and old_mnt is dereferenced by IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE/check_mnt/has_locked_children before any NULL or type validation. If a caller ever passes a path whose ->mnt is NULL or not a real vfsmount, this becomes a NULL/wild dereference (CWE-476). In practice clone_private_mount's callers always pass a fully resolved struct path, so path->mnt is a valid pinned mount — the contract is a caller-side precondition, not an in-function check. The invariant to confirm is that every caller resolves path via kern_path()/user_path or equivalent and holds a reference so path->mnt is non-NULL and stable for the duration.

The new_mnt->mnt_ns assignment happens AFTER up_read at line 18. This is safe only because the CL_PRIVATE clone is brand-new and not yet linked into any namespace or propagation tree, so no concurrent walker can observe it before mnt_ns is set — there is no lock protecting the field here. The lifetime contract is that the returned longterm mount is destroyed via kern_unmount*() (per the comment), not through the normal namespace umount path; a caller that instead attaches it to a real namespace, or fails to kern_unmount it, causes either a mismatched mnt_ns accounting or a mount leak. The invariant to confirm downstream is that every consumer of the returned vfsmount pairs it with kern_unmount and never grafts an MNT_NS_INTERNAL mount into a live namespace.

Lock-balance audit of every exit. Success path: down_read (line 7) ... up_read (line 18) — balanced. Error-from-clone path (line 21): lock already released at line 18, so returning without unlocking is correct, not a leak. Reject paths (goto invalid from lines 9/12/15): reach line 29 up_read exactly once. There is no path that unlocks twice or returns while still holding namespace_sem. The failure mode this guards against — a namespace_sem leak (system-wide mount deadlock) or a double up_read (lock underflow) — does not occur in this arrangement; the split of up_read across line 18 (non-error) and line 29 (error label) is the thing to confirm stays balanced if the code is refactored.

summary: This is the hardened ('fixed') version of clone_private_mount() from the Linux VFS namespace code. It gates a user-influenced mount clone behind three checks under namespace_sem — unbindable, in-namespace (check_mnt), and no-locked-children — before producing a CL_PRIVATE longterm clone, and it balances the lock correctly across all exits. No exploitable defect is visible; the entire security posture rests on the correctness of those three gates (especially has_locked_children) and on callers upholding the path validity and kern_unmount lifetime contracts. Findings below are low/informational and latent (they live in the un-shown helpers or the caller), documenting the contracts an attacker would target rather than a bug present in this snippet.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point whose path argument is user-influenced; its return is an ERR_PTR-or-object contract callers must honor.
Unconditional container_of + later deref; NULL/validity is a caller precondition (latent CWE-476).
Uninitialised local assigned only by clone_mnt() before any use; no security contract of its own.
Lock balance and coverage across success/error/reject exits is a correctness contract; verified balanced but flagged as the thing to keep balanced.
Access-control gate enforcing make-unbindable policy; bypass/reorder is a policy-bypass class.
Namespace-membership gate; core to isolation, part of the same access-control contract.
The central CWE-200 defence added by the fix; correctness of subtree coverage is the primary contract.
Called under the lock after all gates pass; its internal safety is out of scope (helper not shown) and it is correctly ordered.
Standard kernel error-pointer handling; lock already released, no leak, no deref of an error pointer.
Set after unlock and encodes the longterm/kern_unmount lifetime contract; misuse by callers is a latent lifetime/accounting risk.
Returns the embedded public vfsmount handle; ownership transfer is documented, no defect.
Single-exit reject path; unlocks exactly once and returns a proper error pointer — correct.

findings.title: Locked-child exposure defence rests entirely on has_locked_children subtree coverage
findings.bug_class: Information exposure via mount-namespace clone (locked-mount bypass)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — has_locked_children() and clone_mnt() in fs/namespace.c
findings.invariant: has_locked_children must evaluate the ENTIRE subtree that clone_mnt(CL_PRIVATE) will copy (all descendants of old_mnt beneath path->dentry), and it must run under namespace_sem so the child set cannot change before clone_mnt at line 17; if any MNT_LOCKED descendant exists the clone must be rejected.
findings.failure_mode: An unprivileged caller (e.g. via overlayfs lowerdir=/upperdir= inside a user namespace) chooses path->dentry at a subtree containing a mount that a more-privileged namespace locked (MNT_LOCKED) to hide the directory underneath. If has_locked_children misses that descendant (wrong root dentry, partial walk) or is removed, clone_private_mount hands back a private clone from which the attacker can detach/step around the locked mount and read the concealed contents — an information disclosure and namespace-escape. This snippet has the gate correctly placed; the residual risk is the helper's correctness.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read has_locked_children(): confirm it recurses over all children of old_mnt rooted at path->dentry and returns true for any MNT_LOCKED child, and that no locked descendant can be reached by the clone that the walk skips.

findings.title: Access-control gates must precede clone_mnt and all be evaluated (unbindable + in-namespace)
findings.bug_class: Improper access control / mount-namespace isolation bypass
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — check_mnt() and the IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE macro; consumers in overlayfs
findings.invariant: Both gates, plus has_locked_children, must be checked while holding namespace_sem and strictly BEFORE clone_mnt() creates the clone; check_mnt must confine old_mnt to the caller's own (non-internal) mount namespace and IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE must honor the make-unbindable flag.
findings.failure_mode: If either gate is dropped or moved after line 17, a caller could clone an unbindable mount (defeating make-unbindable policy) or a mount not in its own namespace (defeating isolation), because the clone would already be materialised before the rejection. As shown the ordering is correct; the finding documents the contract a refactor must preserve.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm check_mnt rejects mounts whose mnt_ns is not the current task's namespace (and the internal/anonymous namespace), and that no code path skips these gates when reaching clone_mnt.

findings.title: old_mnt dereferenced from path->mnt without in-function NULL/type validation
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — all callers of clone_private_mount()
findings.invariant: Every caller must pass a fully resolved struct path with a non-NULL, reference-held path->mnt that is a genuine embedded vfsmount, so that real_mount() and the subsequent gate dereferences of old_mnt are valid.
findings.failure_mode: real_mount is container_of arithmetic and old_mnt is dereferenced immediately by IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE/check_mnt/has_locked_children with no NULL guard. A caller passing a path with ->mnt == NULL (or a non-mount pointer) triggers a NULL/wild dereference and oops. Not reachable from the correct callers, hence low; it is a precondition, not a defect in this snippet.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm every caller resolves path via kern_path/user_path (or holds an existing pinned path) so path->mnt is always a valid, referenced vfsmount before calling clone_private_mount.

findings.title: Returned mount encodes MNT_NS_INTERNAL longterm lifetime contract (kern_unmount only)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — consumers of the returned vfsmount
findings.invariant: The returned MNT_NS_INTERNAL mount is a kernel-internal longterm mount: it must be released with kern_unmount*() and must never be grafted into a live user mount namespace; the mnt_ns write is safe only because the fresh CL_PRIVATE clone is not yet visible to any concurrent walker at line 24 (post-unlock).
findings.failure_mode: A consumer that forgets to kern_unmount the returned mount leaks it; one that attaches an MNT_NS_INTERNAL mount into a real namespace corrupts mnt_ns accounting and umount propagation. No defect in this function — the risk is downstream and the tag exists precisely to signal the lifetime rule.
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace each caller's use of the returned vfsmount and confirm it is paired with kern_unmount*() and never inserted into a user namespace mount tree.

audit_checklist: has_locked_children(): confirm it walks the full subtree rooted at path->dentry under old_mnt and returns true for ANY MNT_LOCKED descendant, so no locked child can leak through the CL_PRIVATE clone (primary CWE-200 gate).

Confirm all three gates (IS_MNT_UNBINDABLE, check_mnt, has_locked_children) execute under namespace_sem and strictly before clone_mnt(), with no reordering or short-circuit that reaches clone_mnt() unchecked.
Verify check_mnt() restricts old_mnt to the caller's own (non-internal) mount namespace, preventing cloning of foreign/anonymous mounts.
Audit lock balance across every exit: down_read (line 7) matched by exactly one up_read on the success path (line 18) and the reject path (line 29); the IS_ERR return at line 21 must not re-unlock.
Confirm every caller passes a resolved, reference-held struct path so real_mount(path->mnt) never dereferences NULL/invalid pointers.
Trace consumers of the returned MNT_NS_INTERNAL vfsmount to ensure each is released via kern_unmount*() and none is attached into a live user mount namespace.
Confirm clone_mnt(..., CL_PRIVATE) does not itself re-enter code that could observe new_mnt before mnt_ns is set, and that CL_PRIVATE keeps the clone out of propagation/peer groups.
